nativeRange.habitat: Small, shallow forest streams and seasonal pools on the western slopes of the Rakhine Yoma (Arakan) mountains. The type locality — the site where the species was first collected and described — is a stream no more than 3 m wide and around 30 cm deep, with clear water, a gentle current, and a bottom of pebbles, rock, and gravel. Water conditions in this region are monsoon-influenced and seasonally variable.

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Microsorum, mosses) with open midwater swimming lanes
driftwood and leaf litter for natural cover
gentle current from a spray bar or powerhead (4-5x tank-volume turnover)

diet.notes: The Panther Danio is primarily insectivorous in the wild, but in the aquarium it readily accepts quality micro-pellets and small flake as a staple diet. Regular supplementation with small live and frozen foods — such as Daphnia, Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp), and micro-bloodworm — tends to improve coloration and helps condition the fish for breeding.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids (rasboras, other small danios)
avoidWith: ornamental long-finned varieties (fin-nipping risk at feeding time)
slow-moving species that cannot compete for food

imageCandidates.notes: Andrewbogott, 2770x925. Sharp full lateral of a live individual on dark background; clearly shows the diagnostic reticulated gold snakeskin pattern on the posterior flank and the danio barbels. Only clean single-specimen CC photo available (schooling shots not on CC sources).

summary: The Panther Danio is a small schooling fish from forest streams in western Myanmar, named for the reticulated gold-and-brown pattern that resembles snakeskin. It tends to be cool-tolerant compared with most tropical danios and is broadly considered beginner-friendly. Keep in groups of at least eight. This species is a confirmed jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential.
careNotes:
The Panther Danio is endemic to small, shallow, monsoon-fed forest streams on the western slopes of the Rakhine Yoma in western Myanmar — a range physically separated from the rest of the country by the Arakan mountain chain. Formally described only in 2009, it remains less frequently traded than the superficially similar Zebra Danio (D. rerio) and Glowlight Danio (D. choprae, [fw-fish-189]), with which it is sometimes confused in the trade. Its distinctive reticulated pattern — dark vertical bars toward the front of the body and rows of small brown spots toward the rear on a gold base — distinguishes it on close inspection.

Water conditions should mirror its cool, clean, well-oxygenated native streams. Temperature can range from 18 to 27 degrees C, and the species is notably cool-tolerant compared with most tropical danios. A pH of 6.8–7.2 and soft water (gH 1–8) are appropriate. Moderate flow at around 4–5 times the tank volume per hour is recommended; a torrent-like current is not required. A tank of at least 60 cm in length and 60 L in volume is workable for a small group, though a larger footprint allows the natural schooling behaviour to be more fully expressed.

Keep the Panther Danio in schools of at least 8 to 10 individuals. Smaller numbers tend to produce skittish behaviour and suppress the lively group shoaling displays that make this fish interesting to watch. It is generally compatible with other small, peaceful species of comparable size. Avoid tank mates with ornamental long fins, as fin-nipping has been reported at feeding time.

This species is a high-risk jumper and can escape through surprisingly small gaps. A very tightly fitting cover is essential — losses to jumping are common when tanks are left even briefly uncovered.
breedingNotes: The Panther Danio is an egg-scatterer with no parental care, typical of the genus. To breed, condition a mixed-sex group of 8 to 12 fish with a varied diet, then move a smaller spawning group — around 2 to 3 females and 3 to 4 males — into a separate tank lined with fine-leaved plants or left bare over a substrate of mesh or marbles. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water works best for spawning (pH 6.8–7.0, gH 1–4, temperature 24–26 degrees C). The mesh or marbles layer is important because adults will readily eat their own eggs if given the chance. Eggs hatch in approximately 36 hours, and the fry become free-swimming around 3 to 4 days after hatching. Feed newly free-swimming fry infusoria, graduating to Artemia nauplii or finely crushed flake as they grow. No specialist equipment is needed.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Brachydanio aesculapii (Danio aesculapii)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rakhine Yoma, western Myanmar), habitat description, water parameters (temperature 22-27 C, pH 6.9-7.1, hardness 18-90 ppm soft), tank size guidance (60x30 cm minimum), diet (insectivorous; live/frozen supplements), breeding (egg-scatterer, ~36 h incubation, free-swimming at day 4), schooling minimum (8-10), escape-risk warning, and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Danio aesculapii
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (max 3.0 cm SL), native range (Rakhine Yoma, south-western Myanmar), habitat (small forest streams, benthopelagic), trophic level 3.0 (consistent with omnivore/insectivore), and IUCN Near Threatened status (2010).

additional.name: Kullander & Fang 2009 — Danio aesculapii, a new species of danio from south-western Myanmar (Zootaxa 2164)
additional.notes: Original species description. Confirms type locality (small stream on western slope of Rakhine Yoma, max 3 m wide, ~30 cm deep, clear water, pebble/rock/gravel substrate), diagnostic colour pattern (6-7 brown vertical bars anteriorly, two rows of small brown spots posteriorly, no D-stripe), and etymology (aesculapii referencing serpentine markings of Aesculapius).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Panther danio, Danio aesculapii
additional.notes: Supplementary trade-hobby source. Confirmed group size recommendation (minimum 6), fin-nipping risk with ornamental long-finned varieties, escape-risk warning (tight-fitting cover essential), and compatible community choices (small cyprinids, tetras, livebearers, rainbowfish, anabantoids, small catfish).

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Danio aesculapii
additional.notes: Wholesale importer trade record. Confirmed country of origin (Myanmar) and traded size (3-4 cm), consistent with FishBase and SeriouslyFish data.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with no parental care. A spawning group is conditioned in a planted or bare tank with soft, slightly acidic water. Eggs hatch in approximately 36 hours, and fry are free-swimming by around day four. A substrate mesh or marbles layer is recommended to protect eggs from adult predation. No specialist equipment is required.
